lesson.menu_title: Present Perfect vs Past Simple — Talking About Experience
lesson.menu_desc: Say what you have done in your career, and what you did at a specific time — two tenses, one clear story — 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 11 — Present Perfect vs Past Simple: Talking About Experience
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Daniela Feitoza | Aula 11 -- Present Perfect vs Past Simple

listenings.text: Hi Daniela, it is Sarah, your English coach. Great rhythm last time. Today, a quick story about my career, so you can hear two tenses working together. I have worked in technology for fifteen years. I started as a junior analyst in 2009, in a small company. That job taught me everything. Since then, I have changed companies three times, and I have learned something new in each one. Two years ago, I moved to my current team, and I have been very happy here. Notice the pattern. When I give a finished time, like in 2009 or two years ago, I use the past simple: I started, I moved. When I talk about my life experience, with no exact time, I use the present perfect: I have worked, I have learned. Have you ever thought about your own story like this? Try it. Talk soon.

Hey Daniela, it is Bruno from the Lisbon office. I want to tell you about a project, because it is a good example of these two tenses. Last year, our team built a new payment system. We finished it in November, and we launched it in December. Those are finished actions, with a clear time, so I use the past simple: we built, we finished, we launched. But the project is not over for me. I have learned so much from it, and I have already started a new version. I have not stopped improving it yet. Do you see the difference? The finished events are past simple. The experience and the recent results, with no exact time, are present perfect. By the way, congratulations on your progress. You have come a long way. See you at the meeting.
